name: Khonsu
description: Khonsu, the ancient Egyptian god of the moon, time, and healing, was revered as a powerful and complex figure. His name translates to 'traveler,' reflecting the moon's nightly journey across the sky. Born from the union of Amun and Mut in Thebes, Khonsu formed a powerful triad with his parents, representing a significant aspect of Theban religious life. Khonsu was often depicted as a young man, sometimes with the head of a falcon, adorned with a lunar disk and crescent moon on his head. He possessed the power to influence the lunar cycles, control time, and offer protection against evil spirits and illnesses. His association with healing stemmed from his ability to drive away negative energies and restore balance. Temples dedicated to Khonsu served as centers for healing and divination, where priests would invoke his name to alleviate suffering and provide guidance. Khonsu's role extended beyond healing; he was also considered a protector of travelers and a guardian of the night. He was believed to have the ability to influence fertility and ensure successful harvests, further solidifying his importance in Egyptian society. Though often depicted as a benevolent deity, Khonsu could also be fierce in his defense of Ma'at (truth and justice), punishing those who disrupted the cosmic order. The worship of Khonsu persisted for centuries, evolving and adapting to changing religious landscapes, ultimately solidifying his place as a vital and enduring figure in the rich tapestry of Egyptian mythology. He embodies the cyclical nature of time, the power of the moon, and the enduring hope for healing and protection.
subtitle: God of the Moon, Time, and Healing

keyMyths.title: The Gambling Game with Thoth
keyMyths.description: Khonsu gambled with Thoth for extra daylight hours, allowing Nut to give birth to her children, defying Ra's decree. This myth explains the creation of five extra days in the Egyptian calendar.

keyMyths.title: The Healing of the Princess of Bekhten
keyMyths.description: Khonsu was sent to Bekhten to heal a princess afflicted by an evil spirit, demonstrating his immense healing powers. This story highlights Khonsu's role as a benevolent healer and protector.

keyMyths.title: Khonsu's Role in the Theban Triad
keyMyths.description: As part of the Theban Triad with Amun and Mut, Khonsu represented the youthful aspect of the divine family. This triad was central to religious life in Thebes, emphasizing the interconnectedness of the deities.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
